research_series.series_name: Postprandial Glucose Excursions, Brain Fog, Fatigue, Mood, and Cognition

patterns.pattern_name: Acute severe hypoglycemia reliably impairs cognitive performance across multiple domains

contexts: healthy adults under experimental clamp conditions

mediator.name: low_blood_glucose_availability
mediator.pathway: reduced circulating glucose availability during hypoglycemic state
mediator.mechanism: acute energy substrate limitation for glucose-dependent neural computation, with compensatory autonomic and stress responses

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: Acute severe hypoglycemia increases likelihood of immediate cognitive impairment across attention, working memory, processing speed, and cognitive flexibility.

hypotheses.reason: Experimental clamp studies show consistent acute cognitive declines during induced hypoglycemia.

evidence.summary: The strongest causal evidence concerns severe hypoglycemia rather than ordinary post-meal glucose spikes.

key_sources.title: Consistent Effects of Hypoglycemia on Cognitive Function in People With or Without Diabetes
key_sources.relevant_finding: Acute hypoglycemia impaired calculation, attention, working memory, processing speed, and cognitive flexibility.
key_sources.limitations: Experimental hypoglycemia may not fully represent all real-world symptomatic states.

limitations: Most direct causal evidence concerns hypoglycemia rather than hyperglycemia.
Real-world symptom perception may moderate functional impact.

patterns.pattern_name: Short-term glycemic variability is associated with poorer global cognition and executive function mainly in type 2 diabetes

contexts: middle-aged and elderly patients with type 2 diabetes

mediator.name: glucose_instability
mediator.pathway: repeated glucose excursions and metabolic instability
mediator.mechanism: possible oxidative stress, vascular stress, disrupted brain functional architecture, or chronic metabolic burden

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: In people with type 2 diabetes, higher short-term glycemic variability is associated with poorer global cognition and executive function.

hypotheses.reason: CGM and meta-analytic evidence show associations independent of HbA1c, but direct causal mediation is rare.

evidence.summary: Evidence is strong associational in T2D but not enough for a universal causal rule.

key_sources.title: Relationship between acute glucose variability and cognitive decline in type 2 diabetes: A systematic review and meta-analysis
key_sources.relevant_finding: Greater glucose variability associated with increased risk of cognitive impairment in T2D.
key_sources.limitations: Mostly observational; causal direction not fully resolved.

key_sources.title: Relationships Between Daily Acute Glucose Fluctuations and Cognitive Performance Among Aged Type 2 Diabetic Patients
key_sources.relevant_finding: Daily acute glucose fluctuations associated with cognitive performance among aged T2D patients.

limitations: Cannot generalize directly to healthy adults.

patterns.pattern_name: Typical meal-induced postprandial glucose spikes do not consistently impair cognition or fatigue in healthy populations

mediator.name: postprandial_glucose_response
mediator.mechanism: ordinary glucose excursion within non-diabetic physiological range

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: In healthy/non-diabetic populations, ordinary post-meal glucose excursions should not be assumed to cause brain fog or cognitive fatigue.

hypotheses.reason: Most RCTs/crossover studies report null findings for attention/fatigue/working memory after high- versus low-GI meals.

evidence.summary: Null or inconsistent findings predominate for healthy/non-diabetic populations.

key_sources.title: Short-term effects of brief stair climbing interruptions on postprandial hyperglycemia during prolonged sitting
key_sources.relevant_finding: No significant differences in attention scores or subjective fatigue compared with controls.
key_sources.limitations: Short-term intervention; may not capture vulnerable subgroups.

key_sources.title: Impact of lunch with carbohydrates differing in glycemic index on children's cognitive functioning in the late postprandial phase
key_sources.relevant_finding: Meal GI differences did not produce consistent cognitive impairment.
key_sources.limitations: Children; specific lunch context; late postprandial phase.

limitations: Brain fog is rarely directly operationalized.

patterns.pattern_name: Subjective awareness of hypo/hyperglycemia may predict daily functioning more than sensor-detected events alone

mediator.name: perceived_symptomatic_glycemic_event
mediator.pathway: subjective symptom perception and behavioral response to glucose events
mediator.mechanism: symptom awareness, worry, behavioral interruption, or autonomic activation may mediate functional impairment

inference_rule.plain_language_rule: In diabetes, person-reported symptomatic glucose events may be more informative for daily functioning than asymptomatic sensor-only glucose events.

hypotheses.reason: Prospective app-based data found stronger daily functioning effects for person-reported hypoglycemia than sensor-detected asymptomatic events.

evidence.summary: Subjective symptom awareness/action may be a key bridge between metabolic event and experienced fatigue/brain fog.

key_sources.title: The impact of hypoglycaemia on daily functioning among adults with diabetes: a prospective observational study using the Hypo-METRICS app
key_sources.relevant_finding: Person-reported hypoglycemia, more than sensor-detected asymptomatic events, associated with worsened daily functioning.

limitations: Does not prove glucose excursion alone causes symptoms.
May reflect perception, anxiety, or behavioral disruption.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Typical postprandial glucose spikes directly cause subjective brain fog/fatigue in healthy people.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Direct evidence is sparse, brain fog is rarely operationalized, and most healthy/non-diabetic RCTs report null or inconsistent effects.

negative_or_uncertain_findings.claim: Postprandial hyperglycemia causes brain fog through a confirmed physiological mediator.
negative_or_uncertain_findings.reason: Few studies test full physiological-mediator-neurobehavioral chains or formal mediation.

high_value_candidate_nodes.definition: A clinically or experimentally significant drop in blood glucose sufficient to produce acute neurobehavioral effects.
Short-window fluctuation in glucose levels, measured by CGM-derived metrics such as MAGE or coefficient of variation.
Person-reported awareness of hypo/hyperglycemic symptoms during or after glucose events.
Ordinary rise in blood glucose after a meal, especially within non-diabetic physiological ranges.

source_verification_queue.title: Consistent Effects of Hypoglycemia on Cognitive Function in People With or Without Diabetes
Relationship between acute glucose variability and cognitive decline in type 2 diabetes
Postprandial plasma glucose excursions and cognitive functioning in aged type 2 diabetics
The impact of hypoglycaemia on daily functioning among adults with diabetes
Short-term effects of brief stair climbing interruptions on postprandial hyperglycemia during prolonged sitting

research_gaps: Direct evidence linking ordinary postprandial glucose excursions to subjective brain fog/fatigue in healthy adults is weak.
Mediation analyses linking glucose excursion to inflammatory, autonomic, or neural mediators are rare.
Threshold magnitude and duration of PPG excursion needed for cognitive effects in non-diabetic populations remains unclear.
Subjective brain fog is poorly operationalized compared with objective cognition tasks.

most_supported_patterns: Acute severe hypoglycemia reliably impairs cognitive performance.
Short-term glycemic variability is associated with poorer cognition mainly in diabetes.
highest_value_patterns_for_inference: Hypoglycemia as a strong acute metabolic-to-cognitive impairment node.
Glycemic variability as a diabetes/metabolic-risk context marker.
Symptomatic event awareness as a bridge between glucose events and daily functioning.
patterns_requiring_caution: Typical postprandial glucose spikes causing brain fog in healthy adults.
Direct causal mediation from postprandial hyperglycemia to subjective fatigue.
recommended_next_queries: Are postprandial inflammatory cytokines or autonomic responses associated with subjective fatigue, sleepiness, or brain fog after meals?
Do gut hormones such as GLP-1, ghrelin, leptin, CCK, and PYY mediate post-meal sleepiness, motivation, and executive function?
Are reactive hypoglycemia symptoms in non-diabetic adults associated with attention, fatigue, mood, or perceived energy?
summary.overall_assessment: The evidence supports hypoglycemia and diabetes-associated glycemic variability as meaningful neurobehavioral signals. Ordinary postprandial glucose spikes should not be treated as a confirmed cause of brain fog in healthy people. For BodyState Mapper, glucose-spike-to-brain-fog should remain hypothesis-only, while hypoglycemia and glycemic variability deserve graph edges with population/context constraints.
